data.title: Institutions: Fed rate cuts may depend on further slowdown in inflation in the second half of the year.
data.description: Seema Shah, chief global strategist at Principal Asset Management, stated that the current situation is insufficient for the Federal Reserve to justify a near-term rate cut. The continued strength of the labor market provides policymakers with a reason to maintain interest rates, and further slowing of inflation in the second half of the year, as the impact of tariffs fades, should reopen the door to further easing. (Jinshi)

data.title: Spot gold rises above $5,000 per ounce
data.description: According to Gate data, spot gold has risen above $5,000 per ounce after the US January overall and core CPI annual rates both declined.

data.title: The market is betting on a 50/50 chance of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates three times this year.
data.description: U.S. Treasury prices rose in response to lower-than-expected inflation figures released on Friday, fueling investor expectations for three Federal Reserve rate cuts in 2026. The yield on the two-year U.S. Treasury note, most sensitive to central bank policy changes, fell as much as 6 basis points to 3.40%, a new low since October last year, before recovering slightly. Following the data release, traders priced in a rate cut of approximately 63 basis points this year—implying a roughly 50% probability of a third 25-basis-point rate cut before the end of the year, on top of the two already priced-in cuts, compared to 58 basis points priced in on Thursday. Earlier this week, traders had already stopped fully pricing in a 25-basis-point rate cut mid-year in response to the January non-farm payroll data, postponing their bets to July. Wall Street banks that had previously predicted a March rate cut have also pushed their expectations back to later in 2026. (Jinshi)

data.title: Peter Brandt: The crypto market may have hit its true bottom in October, with prices likely to fluctuate upwards in the short term.
data.description: Peter Brandt, the renowned trader and chart analyst who successfully predicted the 2018 Bitcoin crash, stated that the Bitcoin market may not have reached its true bottom yet, suggesting that "the real bottom may not appear until October 2026." He previously predicted that Bitcoin could fall to the $60,000 area in the third quarter of 2026. On February 6th, Bitcoin briefly fell to around $62,700. Brandt believes that the price may fluctuate upwards in the short term, but could still fall back to the $50,000 high range within the year. Meanwhile, Arthur Hayes pointed out that Ethereum's price may continue to trade sideways in its current range until dollar liquidity improves. As of press time, Ethereum was trading at approximately $1,941, a drop of over 40% in the past 30 days. However, Michaël van de Poppe, founder of MN Trading Capital, believes that Ethereum is currently in an attractive entry point, emphasizing that stablecoin trading volume has increased by approximately 200% over the past 18 months. (Cointelegraph)

data.title: Canada's Bank Regulator Defends Capital Requirements Amid Criticism
data.description: Peter Routledge, head of Canada's banking regulatory authority, has responded to criticisms regarding the capital requirements imposed on the nation's largest banks. Bloomberg posted on X, Routledge emphasized that the existing regulations are in a 'Goldilocks zone,' suggesting they are balanced and appropriate. The remarks come amid ongoing debates about the financial burdens faced by major lenders in Canada. Routledge's comments aim to reassure stakeholders that the regulatory framework is neither too stringent nor too lenient, ensuring stability within the banking sector.

data.title: Analyst Highlights Moderate Inflation Trends in Canada
data.description: Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce Capital Markets analyst Ali Jaffery has noted that the year-on-year growth rates for overall inflation and core inflation stand at 2.4% and 2.5%, respectively. According to Jin10, these figures align closely with the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation measure, the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index, which is moving towards policymakers' target of 2%. Historically, the Consumer Price Index (CPI) tends to average about 0.5 percentage points higher than the PCE. Today's data indicates that annual inflation pressures remain moderate, as businesses continue to absorb tariff costs, housing costs decline, and a weak labor market suppresses wage pressures.

data.title: Sao Paulo Faces Severe Climate Challenges Amid Flash Floods and Drought
data.description: Sao Paulo is experiencing its most significant climate-induced stress test in over ten years, with deadly flash floods occurring alongside a severe drought that is causing water shortages. Bloomberg posted on X, highlighting the city's struggle to manage these extreme weather conditions. The combination of heavy rainfall and prolonged dry spells has put immense pressure on the city's infrastructure and resources, leading to widespread disruption and challenges for residents and authorities alike. Efforts are underway to mitigate the impact and address the urgent needs arising from these environmental challenges.

data.title: Principal Asset Management's Seema Shah on Fed's Interest Rate Strategy
data.description: Principal Asset Management's Chief Global Strategist, Seema Shah, stated that the current economic conditions do not justify an immediate interest rate cut by the Federal Reserve. According to Jin10, the robust labor market supports policymakers in maintaining the current interest rates. As the impact of tariffs diminishes, further easing of inflation in the latter half of the year could pave the way for more accommodative policies.

data.title: Analysts: US CPI data provides support for the Federal Reserve to continue cutting interest rates.
data.description: Adam Button, an analyst at the US financial website InvestingLive, commented on the US CPI data, noting that after the data release, the market's pricing in a slightly dovish stance from the Federal Reserve shifted, causing the dollar to weaken and S&P 500 futures to erase earlier losses. It's worth noting that due to the government shutdown causing the absence of October CPI data, November data collection started later than usual, covering more seasonal holiday discounts. Economists generally warned that these confounding factors may have artificially suppressed the readings. Meat prices were a prominent focus, surging 8.9% year-on-year, the largest increase since 2022, with fresh ground beef rising nearly 15%. Although this lower-than-expected report was welcomed by the market and provided support for continued Fed rate cuts, analysts emphasized that the December report will reveal a clearer picture of underlying inflation trends. (Jinshi)

data.title: Americans Continue to Pay High Prices Despite Economic Challenges
data.description: Bloomberg posted on X that Americans are still willing to pay high prices, indicating resilience in consumer spending despite ongoing economic challenges. This trend suggests that consumer confidence remains strong, even as inflationary pressures persist. Analysts are closely monitoring this behavior to understand its implications for the broader economy. The willingness to spend at elevated levels could have significant impacts on economic forecasts and policy decisions.

data.title: Goldman Sachs still expects the Fed to cut rates twice this year, with the next cut in June.
data.description: Lindsay Rosner, head of multi-sector fixed income at Goldman Sachs Asset Management, said that given the January CPI data wasn't as strong as feared, the Fed's "normalization" rate-cutting path seems clearer. This will depend on whether the job market continues to show signs of improvement, as the FOMC is highly sensitive to weakness in the labor market. We still expect the Fed to cut rates twice this year, with the next cut in June. (Jinshi)

data.title: AI Disruption Raises Concerns in Private Credit Lending
data.description: A recent analysis of numerous loans from seven private credit lenders has revealed inconsistencies in the labeling of software firms. Bloomberg posted on X, highlighting that this practice is causing renewed scrutiny over the industry's vulnerability as artificial intelligence continues to transform markets and unsettle investors. The findings suggest that the evolving landscape of AI is prompting a reevaluation of risk exposure within the private credit sector.

data.title: Congo President Agrees to Ceasefire with M23 Rebels
data.description: Democratic Republic of Congo President Felix Tshisekedi has agreed in principle to a ceasefire with the M23 rebels, who are supported by Rwanda and control significant areas in the eastern part of the country. Bloomberg posted on X, highlighting the ongoing conflict that has affected the region for years. The agreement aims to bring stability to the area, which has been plagued by violence and displacement. The ceasefire is seen as a crucial step towards peace, although the implementation details and timeline remain unclear. The international community has been closely monitoring the situation, urging both parties to adhere to the agreement and work towards a lasting resolution.

data.title: No Immediate Plans for Federal Reserve Chair Nomination, Says Senator Tillis
data.description: U.S. Republican Senator Tillis announced that there are currently no plans to advance any nominee for the Federal Reserve Chair position. According to NS3.AI, he highlighted the significance of ongoing hearings aimed at concluding investigations into Fed Chair Jerome Powell. This development indicates a temporary halt in decisions regarding Fed leadership amidst political examination.

data.title: Airline Fares Highlight Consumer Discretionary Spending
data.description: Airline fares have become a focal point in discussions about consumer discretionary spending. Bloomberg posted on X, highlighting how these fares illustrate the choices consumers make in allocating their budgets. The story underscores the broader economic implications of consumer spending patterns, particularly in the travel sector. As airline prices fluctuate, they serve as a barometer for consumer confidence and economic health, reflecting how individuals prioritize their expenditures in varying economic climates.

data.title: Economic Challenges Highlighted Amid Personal Struggles
data.description: Bloomberg posted on X, emphasizing the importance of acknowledging personal struggles during economic hardships. Experts Jonathan Levin, Allison Schrager, and Jose Torres discussed the significance of listening to individuals who express difficulties in the current economic climate. They stressed that dismissing these concerns can overlook critical economic indicators and personal impacts. The conversation highlights the need for empathy and understanding in addressing economic challenges.

data.title: Expert Analysis: Tariff Impact Reflected in U.S. Apparel Price Increase
data.description: Analyst Enda Curran has commented on the recent U.S. Consumer Price Index (CPI) data, noting a slight increase of 0.3% in apparel prices. According to Jin10, this sector is another industry affected by tariffs, indicating signs of tariff pass-through. The analysis highlights the broader economic implications of trade policies on consumer prices.

data.title: US January CPI rose less than expected; Federal Reserve may hold rates steady.
data.description: The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported Friday that the Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose 0.2% month-over-month in January, slightly lower than December's 0.3% increase and also below economists' expectations of 0.3%. Excluding volatile food and energy prices, the core CPI rose 0.3% month-over-month, slightly higher than December's 0.2% increase. Year-over-year, the CPI rose 2.4%, a slowdown from December's 2.7%, mainly due to the high base effect from last year; the core CPI rose 2.5% year-over-year, lower than December's 2.6%. The January report included, for the first time, an update to the seasonal adjustment factor reflecting price changes in 2025. Economists pointed out that the core CPI data in January often exceeds expectations because the Bureau of Labor Statistics' model fails to fully account for one-off price increases at the beginning of the year. This month's increase may reflect both this year-over-year effect and the transmission effect of Trump's broad tariffs. Despite the slowing inflation, a stabilizing labor market may allow the Federal Reserve to keep interest rates unchanged for some time. Economists predict that inflation may rebound temporarily this year due to the transmission of import tariffs and the depreciation of the US dollar last year. (Jinshi)

data.title: Spot gold was last quoted at $4,987 per ounce, while the US dollar index (DXY) rebounded.
data.description: According to Gate data, spot gold's gains later retreated, last trading at $4,987 per ounce; the US dollar index (DXY) rebounded, last trading at 96.93.

data.title: Expert Analysis: Housing Prices Drive U.S. CPI Increase in January
data.description: Analyst Enda Curran has highlighted the impact of housing prices on the U.S. Consumer Price Index (CPI) data for January. According to Jin10, the housing inflation index rose by 0.2% during the month, making it the largest contributor to the overall CPI increase. This development underscores the significant role of housing costs in shaping inflation trends in the United States.
